subject: Criminal Law
lesson_title: Aiding, Abetting, Counselling and Procuring (s.7(1) Criminal Law Act 1997)

questions.question: Section 7(1) of the Criminal Law Act 1997 governs secondary participation in Irish criminal law. What does s.7(1) provide?

options.A: A person who aids, abets, counsels or procures another to commit an offence is guilty of a separate and lesser offence of facilitation, carrying a reduced sentence
options.B: Any person who aids, abets, counsels or procures the commission of an indictable offence is liable to be tried, indicted and punished as a principal offender — secondary participants are treated in law as if they were the principal
options.C: A person who aids, abets, counsels or procures is only liable where the principal offence is actually completed — an attempt by the principal does not trigger secondary liability
options.D: A person is only liable as a secondary party where they are physically present at the scene of the offence

questions.explanation: Section 7(1) Criminal Law Act 1997 provides that any person who aids, abets, counsels or procures the commission of an indictable offence is liable to be tried, indicted and punished as a principal offender. This is the foundational provision of secondary participation in Irish criminal law — it collapses the traditional distinction between principals and accessories (at least for liability purposes): secondary parties are treated as if they themselves committed the offence. The four modes of participation — aid, abet, counsel, procure — are distinct concepts, though they are typically charged conjunctively. Each has its own meaning: aiding (assistance), abetting (encouragement at the scene), counselling (advising/encouraging before the offence), and procuring (bringing about through efforts). Option A incorrectly creates a lesser facilitation offence. Option C incorrectly requires completion of the principal offence. Option D incorrectly requires physical presence — absentees can aid, counsel or procure.

questions.question: In AG's Reference No.1 of 1975 [1975] CA (England and Wales), the court considered whether the mere presence of a bystander at the scene of an offence constitutes abetting. What did the court hold?

options.A: Mere presence at the scene of an offence is sufficient to constitute abetting — a bystander who witnesses a crime and does not intervene is guilty as an abettor
options.B: Mere presence at the scene of a crime, without more, is insufficient to constitute aiding, abetting, counselling, or procuring; there must be some active participation, encouragement, or assistance; however, presence accompanied by prior agreement or wilful encouragement can constitute abetting
options.C: Presence at the scene is only relevant to aiding — it cannot establish abetting, counselling, or procuring
options.D: The accused must have verbally encouraged the principal for presence to constitute abetting

questions.explanation: AG's Reference No.1 of 1975 [1975] CA is the leading English authority on what constitutes secondary participation, applied in Ireland. The court confirmed that mere presence at the scene of a crime — without active participation, encouragement, or prior agreement — is insufficient to constitute aiding, abetting, counselling, or procuring. The classic example is a bystander who witnesses a crime but does nothing: they are not criminally liable as a secondary party. However, the court also confirmed that presence accompanied by: (a) prior agreement to commit the offence; or (b) wilful encouragement or assistance at the scene — can constitute abetting. The 'mere presence' rule prevents the criminalisation of bystanders while ensuring that those who actively support an offence cannot hide behind the fact that they did not physically commit the crime. AG's Reference No.1 is also important for its treatment of procuring (see Q03). Option A incorrectly criminalises mere bystanders. Option C unjustifiably limits presence to the aiding mode only. Option D creates a verbal encouragement requirement that is not the test.

questions.question: In AG's Reference No.1 of 1975 [1975] CA (England and Wales), the court also considered the concept of 'procuring.' A person surreptitiously added alcohol to the principal's drink, causing the principal (unknowingly) to drive over the legal limit. Was this person guilty of procuring the principal's offence of drink driving?

options.A: No — procuring requires the consent and knowledge of the principal; the principal in this case had no knowledge that he was over the limit, so he could not have been procured
options.B: Yes — the court held that to 'procure' means to produce by endeavour; the person who spiked the drink produced the offence (drink driving over the legal limit) by their deliberate efforts; procuring does not require the consent or knowledge of the principal
options.C: No — procuring requires that the procurer communicated their plan to the principal before the offence
options.D: Yes — but the person was guilty of the principal offence of drink driving, not of procuring; the procurer becomes the actual offender in such cases

questions.explanation: AG's Reference No.1 of 1975 [1975] CA established the definition of 'procure' as meaning to 'produce by endeavour.' The person who spiked the drink produced the drink driving offence — the offence would not have occurred but for their deliberate act of adding alcohol. Crucially, the court confirmed that procuring does not require the consent or knowledge of the principal. The principal in this case had no idea he was over the legal limit — he had not agreed to commit drink driving. Yet the procurer's deliberate act caused the offence to occur. This makes procuring unique among the four modes of secondary participation: the other three (aiding, abetting, counselling) generally require some communication with the principal, but procuring can be done without the principal's knowledge. Option A incorrectly requires principal knowledge/consent. Option C adds a prior communication requirement. Option D incorrectly makes the procurer the principal — they remain a secondary party under s.7(1) CLA 1997.

questions.question: In R v Coney [1882], the accused attended a prize fight (illegal bare-knuckle boxing match). The question arose whether spectators who voluntarily attended were guilty as abettors. What principle did this case establish?

options.A: All spectators at an illegal event are automatically guilty as abettors by virtue of their attendance
options.B: Voluntary presence at an illegal event can constitute abetting if it provides encouragement to the principal — the jury must determine whether the accused's presence actually encouraged the commission of the offence; presence that encourages or emboldens the principal can constitute abetting
options.C: Spectators at an illegal event are only guilty as abettors if they placed bets or otherwise financially supported the principal
options.D: The case held that mere passive spectatorship can never constitute abetting — only active assistance constitutes criminal participation

questions.explanation: R v Coney [1882] is the foundational case on presence as abetting. The court held that voluntary presence at an illegal activity can constitute abetting where that presence provides encouragement and support to the principal. The fact that spectators were watching the illegal fight could encourage the fighters to continue. However, the court was careful to note that this was a question for the jury — not all presence at an illegal event automatically constitutes abetting. The jury must determine whether the accused's presence actually did encourage or embolden the principal. This case is cited alongside AG's Reference No.1 to illustrate the spectrum: mere passive presence (AG's Reference) is insufficient; deliberate encouraging presence at an illegal event (Coney) can cross the line into abetting. The distinction depends on the facts and the degree to which the presence provides encouragement. Options A and D take absolutist positions at opposite ends. Option C invents a financial participation requirement.

questions.question: In DPP v Ryan [2011] (Ireland), the accused was armed and present when the principal attacked the victim. The court addressed whether the accused's armed presence constituted secondary participation. What did the case confirm?

options.A: Armed presence at the scene of an offence is per se equivalent to being a principal — the accused automatically becomes a co-principal if armed
options.B: Armed presence at the scene of an offence can constitute secondary participation — the accused's visible armament could provide encouragement or intimidation that facilitates the principal's offence; the jury should consider whether the presence actually provided encouragement or assistance to the principal
options.C: Armed presence is irrelevant to secondary participation unless the accused used the weapon during the offence
options.D: The case held that the accused was not guilty as a secondary party because the victim was attacked by the principal alone; secondary participation requires joint physical action

questions.explanation: DPP v Ryan [2011] illustrates the 'armed presence' variant of abetting in Irish law. The court confirmed that an accused who is present at the scene of an offence while visibly armed can be providing significant encouragement and support to the principal — even without using the weapon. The armed presence may: intimidate potential opposition or victims who might otherwise resist; demonstrate to the principal that support is available; and embolden the principal to proceed. Whether the armed presence actually provided encouragement or assistance is a jury question on the facts. This case is cited as Irish authority applying the Coney principle — that presence can constitute abetting where it provides genuine encouragement — to the specific context of armed presence. Option A incorrectly makes armed presence automatically equivalent to co-principalship. Option C incorrectly requires actual weapon use. Option D incorrectly requires joint physical action.

questions.question: In DPP v Madden [2010] (Ireland), the accused drove the principal to and from the scene of a robbery. What principle did the case address regarding the provision of transport?

options.A: Driving the principal to a crime scene cannot constitute secondary participation — the driver is too remote from the offence to be liable
options.B: Providing transport to and from a crime can constitute secondary participation in the form of aiding — where the accused drives the principal to the scene and waits, knowing the offence to be committed, their facilitation of the offence can make them an aider under s.7(1) CLA 1997
options.C: Providing transport is only relevant as a factor in sentencing, not as a basis for criminal liability
options.D: The accused can only be liable as a secondary party if they knew the specific offence to be committed — general knowledge of criminal activity is insufficient

questions.explanation: DPP v Madden [2010] confirms that providing transport for the commission of an offence can constitute aiding under s.7(1) Criminal Law Act 1997. The accused who drives a principal to and from a robbery facilitates the offence — without the transport, the robbery might not have been possible or the principal might not have escaped. This is active assistance (aiding), not merely passive presence. The case applies the general principle that any act that facilitates, assists or enables the commission of an offence can found secondary liability, provided the accused has the requisite knowledge and intent. The driver's knowledge that a robbery (or other offence) was being committed is an essential element — and the Irish courts have examined what level of knowledge is sufficient (see DPP v Egan [2009] on awareness). Options A and C incorrectly remove transport from the scope of secondary liability. Option D raises a valid point about knowledge level — but 'general knowledge' framing is misleading; the driver must know a crime is being committed, and in Madden the facts established this.

questions.question: In R v Calhaem [1985] CA (England and Wales), the accused hired a contract killer to murder her rival. The killer killed the victim but claimed he had decided to kill her independently, in a frenzy. The question was whether the accused had 'counselled' the murder even if the killer's act was not directly connected to the counselling. What did the court hold?

options.A: Counselling requires that the offence be committed as a direct and immediate result of the counselling — if the principal acts for their own independent reasons, the counsellor is not liable
options.B: Counselling does not require the principal to be directly motivated by the counselling at the moment of commission — it is sufficient that the principal committed the offence within the scope of the act counselled; the principal's mixed or additional motives do not exonerate the counsellor
options.C: The accused was not guilty of counselling because the killer claimed he acted in a frenzy — an independent mental state by the principal severs the causal link required for counselling liability
options.D: Counselling requires the counsellor to be physically present at the scene of the offence — an absent counsellor can only be liable for procuring, not counselling

questions.explanation: R v Calhaem [1985] CA established that for counselling, it is not necessary that the principal's act be directly and exclusively motivated by the counselling. The court held that where the principal commits the offence within the scope of the act counselled, the counsellor is liable — even if the principal had additional or independent motivations at the time of commission. In Calhaem, the killer murdered the victim (as counselled) — the fact that he claimed to have been in a frenzy and to have acted partly for his own reasons did not break the causal connection required for counselling liability. The offence was committed within the scope of what was counselled (murder of the victim). Calhaem thus prevents accused persons from escaping liability as counsellors by pointing to some independent motivation by the principal. Option A adds a direct motivation requirement that Calhaem rejected. Option C incorrectly treats the killer's claimed frenzy as severing liability. Option D incorrectly requires physical presence for counselling.

questions.question: In DPP for NI v Maxwell [1978] HOL, Maxwell drove gang members to a pub they planned to attack. He did not know whether they would use bombs, guns, or knives. What principle did the case establish about knowledge required for secondary liability?

options.A: Maxwell was not guilty as a secondary party because he did not know the specific method of attack that would be used — specific knowledge is required for secondary liability
options.B: Maxwell was guilty — a secondary party's knowledge that a crime from a range of possible crimes would be committed is sufficient; the secondary party need not know the specific offence that will be committed, as long as the offence actually committed is within the range of offences they contemplated
options.C: Maxwell was not guilty because he did not participate in the actual attack — secondary liability requires physical participation in the principal offence
options.D: Maxwell was guilty only of the lesser offence of membership of an illegal organisation — he could not be liable as a secondary party to the specific attack

questions.explanation: DPP for NI v Maxwell [1978] HOL is the leading authority on the 'range of crimes' principle for secondary participation. Maxwell drove gang members to a pub knowing they intended to attack it — but he was uncertain of the specific method (guns, bombs, knives). The House of Lords held that Maxwell was guilty as a secondary party to the attack. The principle established is that a secondary party who knows that a crime will be committed from a range of possible crimes is liable for whichever offence from that range is actually committed. Maxwell's knowledge that some form of violent attack would occur was sufficient — he did not need to know whether it would be a bombing, shooting, or stabbing. This prevents secondary parties from creating doubt by claiming ignorance of the specific method. The actual offence (in Maxwell, an explosive attack) must fall within the range of offences the secondary party contemplated. Option A incorrectly requires specific knowledge of the method. Option C requires physical participation. Option D invents a lesser charge.

questions.question: In R v Giannetto [1997] CA (England and Wales), a husband allegedly killed his wife. The question arose whether he was a principal or a secondary party — or both. A witness claimed the husband had responded to news of someone's intention to kill his wife with 'Good, I'll give you a thousand pounds.' What principle did this case illustrate?

options.A: This conduct was insufficient for secondary liability — secondary participation requires direct assistance, not mere expressions of approval
options.B: Even a small degree of encouragement — such as a 'pat on the back' or an expression of approval — can constitute secondary participation; where a person encourages a criminal plan, even by minimal words or gestures, they can be liable as a secondary party; the court confirmed there was a spectrum from enthusiastic encouragement to the most minimal encouragement
options.C: The accused's words amounted to procuring only — verbal encouragement without physical assistance cannot constitute any other mode of secondary participation
options.D: The accused's liability depended on whether he actually paid the thousand pounds — conditional encouragement that is not fulfilled cannot found secondary liability

questions.explanation: R v Giannetto [1997] CA established that the threshold of encouragement required for secondary liability is low. The court held that even a 'pat on the back' — a trivial physical gesture of approval — or an expression of enthusiasm for another's stated plan to commit a crime could constitute secondary participation. The husband's 'Good, I'll give you a thousand pounds' was clearly encouragement of the plan to murder his wife, constituting counselling or abetting. The case illustrated that secondary liability extends from the most active assistance down to the minimum encouragement — any voluntary, knowing encouragement of the criminal plan suffices. This prevents persons from escaping liability by arguing their encouragement was too minimal or casual. The case also illustrated that an accused may be liable as either a principal or a secondary party, or possibly both — where it cannot be established which role they played, the jury may be directed on both alternatives. Option A incorrectly requires direct assistance. Option C unjustifiably limits verbal encouragement to procuring only. Option D conditions liability on actual payment — not required.
